---
title: "Untitled"
format: html
---

Class Exercise 0.

a. 5 rows.

b. 6 rows.

c. 8 rows.

d. 3 rows (A, C, E)

e. 3 rows.

f. 2 rows.

Class Exercise 1. To use bind_cols(), the two data frames must have cases in the same exact order.

Class Exercise 2.

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
slumajors_df <- read_csv(here("data/SLU_Majors_17_21.csv"))
collegemajors_df <- read_csv(here("data/college-majors.csv"))
slumajors_df |> glimpse()
collegemajors_df |> glimpse()

left_join(slumajors_df, collegemajors_df,
          join_by(Major == Major))
## this is a problem: when joining, the keys have to match
## exactly.
```





Your Turn 1.

a. true

b. true. For both a and b, an inner_join only returns rows with matching keys in both data frames while a left_ and full_ join return at least these rows and possibly others.

c. false: see YT 2 a and e for a counterexample.

Your Turn 2.

a. 6 rows (A, A, C, C, F, F)

b. 8 rows (A, A, B, C, C, D, D, D)

c. 10 rows (A, A, B, C, C, D, D, D, F, F)

d. 4 rows (A, A, C, C).

e. 4 rows (A, A, C, C, yvar is not kept at all)

f. 2 rows (F, F).
